summary: Julia Evans shares her impressions of the Julia programming language after a week of use during Hacker School. She highlights its appealing combination of Python-like ease and C-like performance, its IPython notebook integration, and the ability to inspect LLVM code for functions. She notes that much of Julia is written in Julia itself, making it accessible for contributors. Her complaints are minor, mostly around REPL startup time and session instability when redefining functions.
key_points: Julia combines Python's friendly syntax with C-like memory layout and performance characteristics
IPython notebook integration via IJulia was a major draw for her interactive workflow
The ability to inspect LLVM intermediate code for any function provides unique transparency into performance
Julia being largely self-hosted means contributors can improve the language without switching to C
The main frustrations are minor REPL issues rather than fundamental language problems
money_quotes: I will never get tired of the fact that Julia is called Julia.
I've been programming in Julia for about a week now. So now is a good time to share my Extreme Julia Expertise.
It's like C. This is kind of the opposite of 'it's like Python'.
So if you want to add to the standard library or improve Julia's type inference, you can do it in Julia!
Having to switch to a different language to make your fast is no fun.
Multiple dispatch is also pretty neat. I may write more about it later, because I'm not able to articulate yet why it gives me nice programming patterns.
key_insight: Julia's power lies in combining the ease of a dynamic scripting language with the performance transparency of a systems language, all while being largely self-hosted.
